Jacob continued cheerfully on his journey after a meaningful time with God at Beth-el. Circumstances led him to the field where his uncle's flocks were being watered. What is said about the care shepherds have for their sheep may remind us of the tender concern our Lord Jesus, the great Shepherd of the sheep, has for his flock, the church; for he is the good Shepherd who knows his sheep and is known by them. The stone at the well's mouth was to secure it; water was scarce, not available for everyone’s use: but separate interests should not prevent us from helping one another. When all the shepherds came together with their flocks, they watered their flocks together as loving neighbors. Kindness in speech has a powerful influence. Jacob was polite to these strangers and found them polite to him.

See Rachel's humility and diligence. No one needs to be ashamed of honest, useful labor, nor should it hinder anyone’s advancement. When Jacob realized this was his relative, he was very willing to serve her. Laban, though not always good-natured, welcomed him and was satisfied with the account Jacob gave of himself. While we avoid being too quick to believe everything that is told us, we must also be careful not to be suspiciously distrustful.

During the month Jacob spent as a guest, he was not idle. Wherever we are, it’s good to employ ourselves in some useful work. Laban wanted Jacob to stay with him. Those who are related but of lower status should not be taken advantage of; it is our duty to reward them. Jacob told Laban about the affection he had for his daughter Rachel. And having no worldly possessions with which to provide for her, he promised seven years’ service. Love makes long and difficult services seem short and easy; hence we read of labor done out of love. If we know how to value heavenly happiness, the sufferings of this present time will be nothing to us. An age of work will be as a few days to those who love God and look forward to Christ’s return. Jacob, who had deceived his father, was now deceived by Laban, his father-in-law, by a similar deception. In this situation, though Laban was unjust, the Lord was righteous. Even the righteous, if they take a wrong step, are sometimes repaid in this way on earth. And many who, like Jacob, were disappointed in their marriage regarding appearance, soon find themselves, to their grief, disappointed in character as well. The choice of that relationship should be made with good advice and careful consideration on both sides. There is reason to believe that Laban’s excuse was not true. His way of settling the matter made things worse. Jacob was drawn into the trouble of having multiple wives. He could not refuse Rachel, because he had already promised to marry her; even less could he refuse Leah. As yet there was no explicit command against marrying more than one wife. It was a sin of ignorance among the patriarchs, but it does not justify such practice now, when God’s will is plainly made known by Divine law and even more fully by our Savior, who taught that only one man and one woman should be joined together.

The names Leah gave her children expressed her respect and regard for both God and her husband. Reuben, meaning See a son, with the thought, Now my husband will love me; Levi, meaning joined, expecting, Now my husband will be joined to me. Mutual affection is both the duty and comfort of marriage; and spouses should strive to please each other. She gratefully acknowledged God’s kind providence in answering her prayers. Whatever supports and comforts us under afflictions or tends to our deliverance from them, God must be recognized for it. Her fourth son she called Judah, meaning praise, saying, Now I will praise the Lord. This was he of whom, as concerning the flesh, Christ came. Whatever is the cause of our rejoicing should also be the reason for our thanksgiving. New blessings should prompt us to thank God for past blessings; Now I will praise the Lord more and better than I have done before. All our praises must center in Christ, both as the subject of them and as the mediator of them. He descended after the flesh from him whose name was “Praise,” and He is our praise. Is Christ formed in my heart? Now I will praise the Lord.
